narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 46.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24 and LSAT is 37.27, while top performers typically rank in much higher deciles like the 6th for both early and later years. The three most influential factors in your prediction were your LSAT score, your Year 3 decile ranking, and your Year 1 decile ranking. Your LSAT score had the strongest positive sway toward passing, as it was solid enough to push back against the overall risk, even if slightly below the average for successful bar takers. In contrast, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 was the biggest driver toward predicting failure, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers who passed. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 2 added further concern, reinforcing a pattern of lower standing compared to the 6th decile average among those who succeeded. Your full-time status aligns well with the 93% of passers who chose that path, and your middle income bracket is common among them at 35%, so those didn't tip the scales much. Overall, your profile stands out as riskier than typical admitted students, whose stronger early grades and test scores align more closely with bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program, which could reshape your profile for future applications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas and reapplying—your full-time commitment is already a great foundation. We encourage you to seek advising from pre-law counselors to build a clearer path ahead.
